domains.description: Tests whether the business has a formally governed source system for leads, opportunities, customers, invoices, renewals, and management KPIs, rather than conflicting CRM exports, finance files, and shadow spreadsheets.
domains.metric_name: Source of Truth & System Architecture score

domains.description: Tests whether users capture required ownership, stage, date, amount, status, and next-step data at the point where work happens, instead of correcting records only when reporting is due.

Tests whether account, contact, deal, invoice, and service records are complete, current, deduplicated, and categorised consistently enough to support funnel, forecast, billing, cash, and retention reporting.
Tests whether the data shows the full lifecycle from lead through qualification, proposal, close, invoicing, payment, onboarding, and renewal, including handoffs, aging, backlog, and drop-off.
Tests whether core management KPIs have approved formulas, inclusion rules, targets, owners, and refresh logic so the same metric means the same thing across dashboards and reports.
Tests whether dashboards and management packs are built from trusted data, focused on decisions and exceptions, and consistent across leadership, managers, and operating teams.
Tests whether named owners, SOPs, review cadence, change control, and issue-resolution routines exist to keep data, KPI logic, and reporting accurate over time.

questions.workflow_objective: Strengthen data quality, reporting trust, governance, and source-of-truth discipline.
questions.question_text: Is there a formally approved system of record for leads, opportunities, customers, invoices, and renewal data, or do different teams rely on different sources?
questions.guidance: Assess how consistently this control exists in live operations and whether reporting can be trusted because of it.
questions.evidence_examples: System inventory; source-owner interviews; reporting source map
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weakness in source of truth & system architecture reduces reporting trust and weakens operational control.
questions.score_1_guidance: Control is largely absent or highly inconsistent.
questions.score_3_guidance: Control exists but is inconsistent or partly manual.
questions.score_5_guidance: Control is defined, reliable, and regularly governed.

findings.finding_text: Leads, opportunities, customers, invoices, and KPI reports are being assembled from conflicting CRM exports, finance files, and shadow spreadsheets, so the business lacks a governed source of truth for core management numbers.
findings.business_impact: Leads, opportunities, customers, invoices, and KPI reports are being assembled from conflicting CRM exports, finance files, and shadow spreadsheets, so the business lacks a governed source of truth for core management numbers.
findings.likely_root_cause: No approved system-of-record policy; fragmented tool ownership; spreadsheet workarounds; mismatched object and field design

findings.finding_text: Some core objects have a defined source, but KPI reporting still depends on manual extracts, local spreadsheets, or inconsistent field mapping across CRM, finance, and service data.
findings.business_impact: Some core objects have a defined source, but KPI reporting still depends on manual extracts, local spreadsheets, or inconsistent field mapping across CRM, finance, and service data.
findings.likely_root_cause: Incomplete source mapping; partial integration coverage; inconsistent field standards; weak lineage documentation

findings.finding_text: The source architecture is mostly reliable, though documentation, cross-system reconciliation, and end-to-end lineage can be strengthened to protect reporting as complexity grows.
findings.business_impact: The source architecture is mostly reliable, though documentation, cross-system reconciliation, and end-to-end lineage can be strengthened to protect reporting as complexity grows.
findings.likely_root_cause: Growth outpacing documentation; limited reconciliation discipline; unclear integration ownership

findings.finding_text: Required fields, owners, dates, and stage or status updates are not being captured when work happens, so records are completed late or inconsistently and reporting loses credibility.
findings.business_impact: Required fields, owners, dates, and stage or status updates are not being captured when work happens, so records are completed late or inconsistently and reporting loses credibility.
findings.likely_root_cause: Missing mandatory fields; weak stage-exit rules; no owner accountability; users updating records after the fact

findings.finding_text: Basic capture standards exist, but teams are inconsistent in how quickly and fully they update pipeline, billing, or customer records across the month.
findings.business_impact: Basic capture standards exist, but teams are inconsistent in how quickly and fully they update pipeline, billing, or customer records across the month.
findings.likely_root_cause: Uneven manager enforcement; partial training; inconsistent field validation; weak month-to-month discipline

findings.finding_title: Capture discipline is strong but can be tightened
findings.finding_text: Capture discipline is generally strong, though exception handling and compliance monitoring can be tightened in high-volume or high-risk workflow steps.
findings.business_impact: Capture discipline is generally strong, though exception handling and compliance monitoring can be tightened in high-volume or high-risk workflow steps.
findings.likely_root_cause: Limited exception monitoring; control fatigue in busy teams; light-touch compliance review

findings.finding_text: Duplicates, missing values, stale statuses, and inconsistent categories across accounts, deals, invoices, or service records are materially distorting funnel, forecast, billing, or customer reporting.
findings.business_impact: Duplicates, missing values, stale statuses, and inconsistent categories across accounts, deals, invoices, or service records are materially distorting funnel, forecast, billing, or customer reporting.
findings.likely_root_cause: No cleansing routine; weak validation rules; uncontrolled updates; poor master-data hygiene

findings.finding_title: Data quality is usable but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Core reporting is possible, but recurring completeness, freshness, or classification issues still force manual reconciliation and reduce confidence in management numbers.
findings.business_impact: Core reporting is possible, but recurring completeness, freshness, or classification issues still force manual reconciliation and reduce confidence in management numbers.
findings.likely_root_cause: Reactive cleanup; no measurable quality thresholds; inconsistent stewardship by record type

findings.finding_title: Data quality is strong with targeted improvement opportunities
findings.finding_text: Data quality is broadly reliable, though targeted controls around anomalies, master-data drift, and exception trends would improve resilience as volume grows.
findings.business_impact: Data quality is broadly reliable, though targeted controls around anomalies, master-data drift, and exception trends would improve resilience as volume grows.
findings.likely_root_cause: Limited anomaly monitoring; master-data ownership not fully matured; controls focused on cleanup more than prevention

findings.finding_title: Workflow visibility is broken in the data
findings.finding_text: The data does not show the full lifecycle from lead to cash and ongoing customer delivery, leaving handoffs, delays, rework, and drop-off largely invisible.
findings.business_impact: The data does not show the full lifecycle from lead to cash and ongoing customer delivery, leaving handoffs, delays, rework, and drop-off largely invisible.
findings.likely_root_cause: Workflow steps not translated into system fields or statuses; missing timestamps; handoff events not captured; teams bypassing official stages

findings.finding_text: Major workflow stages are visible, but key blind spots remain in handoffs, aging, backlog, rework, or exception tracking across critical processes.
findings.business_impact: Major workflow stages are visible, but key blind spots remain in handoffs, aging, backlog, rework, or exception tracking across critical processes.
findings.likely_root_cause: Partial process mapping; incomplete timestamping; queue states not captured; inconsistent use of workflow fields

findings.finding_title: Workflow visibility is strong but can be made more analytical
findings.finding_text: Workflow visibility is strong, though reporting can become more diagnostic by highlighting stalled work, abnormal cycle time, and stage-level risk.
findings.business_impact: Workflow visibility is strong, though reporting can become more diagnostic by highlighting stalled work, abnormal cycle time, and stage-level risk.
findings.likely_root_cause: Summary views dominate; limited exception design; weak comparative cycle-time analytics

findings.finding_text: Core KPIs are not governed consistently: definitions, formulas, targets, or owners differ across dashboards, spreadsheets, and team reports, making management reporting unreliable.
findings.business_impact: Core KPIs are not governed consistently: definitions, formulas, targets, or owners differ across dashboards, spreadsheets, and team reports, making management reporting unreliable.
findings.likely_root_cause: No KPI dictionary; inconsistent numerator/denominator logic; unclear ownership; thresholds not agreed

findings.finding_text: A core KPI set exists, but some metrics still use inconsistent logic, thresholds, time periods, or ownership across reports and teams.
findings.business_impact: A core KPI set exists, but some metrics still use inconsistent logic, thresholds, time periods, or ownership across reports and teams.
findings.likely_root_cause: Incomplete metric governance; weak alignment between team reports and leadership packs; partial target-setting discipline

findings.finding_title: KPI framework is strong with room for refinement
findings.finding_text: KPI logic is largely controlled, though forward-looking indicators, threshold design, and benchmarking can be improved to make the layer more actionable.
findings.business_impact: KPI logic is largely controlled, though forward-looking indicators, threshold design, and benchmarking can be improved to make the layer more actionable.
findings.likely_root_cause: Focus on lagging indicators; limited benchmark context; thresholds not always tied to action

findings.finding_title: Reporting is manual and not decision-ready
findings.finding_text: Leadership reporting depends on manual manipulation, duplicated reports, or poorly structured dashboards, so decisions are being made from delayed or conflicting information.
findings.business_impact: Leadership reporting depends on manual manipulation, duplicated reports, or poorly structured dashboards, so decisions are being made from delayed or conflicting information.
findings.likely_root_cause: Poor report design; weak data trust; version sprawl; no clear management-report standard

findings.finding_text: Reporting is useful in places, but remains uneven in timeliness, consistency, and decision-focus across stakeholder groups.
findings.business_impact: Reporting is useful in places, but remains uneven in timeliness, consistency, and decision-focus across stakeholder groups.
findings.likely_root_cause: Report sprawl; weak refresh discipline; dashboards built for visibility rather than decisions; manual preparation burden

findings.finding_title: Reporting is strong but can be made more actionable
findings.finding_text: Reporting is broadly trusted, though it can be made more action-led through clearer exception views, role-based layouts, and early-warning signals.
findings.business_impact: Reporting is broadly trusted, though it can be made more action-led through clearer exception views, role-based layouts, and early-warning signals.
findings.likely_root_cause: Limited management-by-exception design; weak proactive alerting; summary-heavy dashboard structure

findings.finding_text: No robust governance model exists to keep data quality, KPI logic, and reporting accuracy under control, with weak ownership and no formal correction routine.
findings.business_impact: No robust governance model exists to keep data quality, KPI logic, and reporting accuracy under control, with weak ownership and no formal correction routine.
findings.likely_root_cause: No governance model; unclear accountability; undocumented operating rules; issues resolved informally

findings.finding_title: Governance exists but is inconsistently applied
findings.finding_text: Governance exists in parts, but accountability, review cadence, change control, and enforcement are inconsistent across teams.
findings.business_impact: Governance exists in parts, but accountability, review cadence, change control, and enforcement are inconsistent across teams.
findings.likely_root_cause: Partial SOP adoption; inconsistent leadership follow-through; governance meetings not linked to action tracking

findings.finding_title: Governance is strong with maturity improvement potential
findings.finding_text: Governance is largely in place, though escalation, control tracking, and continuous improvement can be strengthened.
findings.business_impact: Governance is largely in place, though escalation, control tracking, and continuous improvement can be strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Limited escalation discipline; control logs not consistently used; governance focused on maintenance more than improvement

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Establish and approve a system-of-record model for leads, opportunities, customers, invoices, and KPI reporting; remove or control shadow sources; and document end-to-end data lineage.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Conduct a source-of-truth mapping exercise across every critical business object — leads, opportunities, customers, invoices, and KPI reports. For each object, identify every system and spreadsheet currently contributing data, determine which one should be the authoritative source, and formally retire or restrict shadow sources. Document the approved system-of-record in a data architecture register. Assign an owner per object who is accountable for accuracy and access control.

This is the foundational step for the entire data improvement programme. Without a clear source of truth, every downstream improvement — data quality, KPI logic, reporting — will be undermined by conflicting inputs. The commercial consequence of inaction is that revenue numbers cannot be reconciled, management decisions are made from inconsistent information, and any AI or automation initiative built on top will inherit the same unreliability.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Standardize source mapping for every critical KPI and reporting object, align cross-system field definitions, and reduce reliance on manual extracts.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Map every field used in critical KPI calculations and pipeline or billing reports to its source system. Where the same field is populated in multiple systems, align on a single canonical definition and field location. Deprecate or control all secondary sources. Publish a field-level data dictionary covering source, owner, refresh frequency, and transformation rule.

Partial source alignment — where some objects are controlled and others are not — creates a false sense of reliability. Management may trust the outputs without realising some figures are still assembled manually from uncontrolled extracts. Standardising field-level source mapping eliminates the root cause of unexplained variances in reporting and reduces the time spent reconciling figures before leadership reviews.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Strengthen architecture resilience by formalising lineage, reconciliation rules, and integration ownership across CRM, finance, service, and BI layers.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Document end-to-end data lineage for all critical reporting objects, including transformation steps, integration handoffs, and reconciliation checkpoints. Assign integration ownership so that when a source system changes, the downstream impact is identified and managed before reports break. Build automated reconciliation checks between source and reporting layers.

At this maturity level, architecture is largely functional but lineage documentation and cross-system reconciliation are typically informal. As transaction volume grows or systems change, undocumented integration dependencies become silent failure points. Formalising lineage now protects the reliability of the data layer through future system changes, team changes, and growth.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Enforce stage-based capture rules so required owner, status, date, amount, and next-step fields are completed when work occurs, supported by hygiene controls and accountability.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Implement stage-gate capture rules in the CRM and operational systems so required fields — owner, status, amount, dates, next steps — cannot be bypassed without explicit exception approval. Run a back-fill exercise to remediate the most critical incomplete records. Establish a weekly compliance report for sales and operations managers showing capture completion rates by team and stage.

Poor capture discipline is one of the most common and most costly data problems in growing businesses. When fields are incomplete, pipeline reports are wrong, forecasting is unreliable, and revenue leakage goes undetected. Every analysis built on that data — from marketing attribution to customer health scoring — inherits the same errors. Fixing capture at the point of entry is faster and cheaper than attempting to correct it downstream.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Tighten capture discipline through validation rules, manager compliance reviews, and role-based training on the fields that drive reporting.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Identify the specific stages, teams, and field types where capture compliance is weakest. Implement targeted validation rules at those points. Run monthly manager reviews of capture rates with defined improvement targets. Introduce role-based training focused on the commercial consequence of missing data, not just the system mechanics of filling in fields.

Inconsistent capture at the developing stage typically reflects a behavioural and accountability gap rather than a system design gap. The fix is a combination of technical controls — validation, alerts — and management discipline, where capture rates become a visible performance indicator that managers track and act on regularly.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Improve capture consistency at scale using exception alerts, targeted refresher training, and tighter monitoring of high-volume workflows.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Add exception monitoring for high-risk or high-volume capture points where compliance slips are most consequential. Build dashboards that surface capture compliance by workflow stage and trigger manager alerts when rates drop below agreed thresholds. Use refresher training to address specific gaps identified in monitoring data rather than generic system training.

At strong maturity, capture discipline is generally working but remains dependent on individual behaviour. Systematic exception monitoring removes that dependency by surfacing problems before they compound. This is especially important in high-growth periods when transaction volume increases faster than management attention can track.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Complete a structured data remediation for duplicates, missing values, stale stages, and inconsistent categories, then implement controls that prevent the same issues from reappearing.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Run a structured data remediation sprint. Export and audit records across accounts, deals, invoices, and service objects. Categorise issues by type — duplicates, missing values, stale statuses, inconsistent classifications. Prioritise the issues most directly affecting revenue reporting, billing accuracy, and customer visibility. Assign remediation ownership and set a target completion date. Simultaneously, implement prevention controls to stop new quality issues entering the database.

Unresolved data quality issues compound over time. What begins as a few duplicate accounts or stale pipeline records becomes a systemic reporting problem that forces teams to maintain their own local spreadsheets. Those spreadsheets then become shadow sources of truth, reintroducing the fragmentation problem. Running a remediation sprint and closing the entry points permanently is faster than tolerating the ongoing cost of working around bad data.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Set measurable quality standards for completeness, freshness, classification, and duplicate prevention, with clear stewardship for each record type.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Define measurable quality standards for each critical data object — completeness threshold, maximum acceptable age, classification consistency rate, duplicate rate. Assign a data steward per domain who is responsible for monitoring against those standards. Run monthly data quality reviews with management. Build these metrics into existing operational reviews rather than creating a separate reporting process.

Data quality at the developing stage typically lacks objective measurement. Without defined standards, quality improvement has no target, degradation has no trigger, and accountability has no anchor. Formalising standards does not require a large technology investment — it requires clear definitions, named owners, and a regular review cadence.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Add proactive quality monitoring for anomalies, master-data drift, and exception trends so reliability holds as transaction volume grows.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Build automated monitoring for anomalies, master-data drift, and exception trends across the highest-risk data objects. Set alert thresholds so stewards and managers are notified when quality metrics move outside acceptable bounds without waiting for the next scheduled review. Run quarterly data quality retrospectives to identify structural causes of recurring issues.

At strong maturity, quality is generally reliable but reactive. Proactive monitoring closes the gap between when a quality issue enters the system and when it is detected. This is particularly important for high-velocity data — pipeline records, billing entries, support tickets — where small degradations accumulate quickly if not surfaced early.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Redesign workflow data capture so lifecycle stages, handoffs, timestamps, backlog, and drop-off are visible from lead through invoicing, payment, onboarding, and renewal.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Redesign data capture for the full commercial and operational lifecycle. Map the journey from first lead touch through to customer delivery and renewal. For each transition point — qualification, proposal, close, onboarding, delivery milestone, billing, renewal — identify what data should be captured, who captures it, and what system it lives in. Build capture at the point of work, not as a retrospective admin task.

When the data does not reflect the full workflow, management cannot see where the business is winning or losing. Deals close in the system but delivery problems are invisible. Customers churn without a visible warning signal. Billing completes but invoice queries or disputes accumulate in inboxes rather than in trackable records. Each of these is a commercial risk that better workflow data would surface in time to act on.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Close workflow blind spots by adding missing handoff fields, aging views, queue controls, and stage-movement reporting across critical processes.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Identify the specific handoff and transition points where data visibility breaks down. Common blind spots include the hand from sales to delivery, the movement from project milestone to billing trigger, and the path from support ticket to churn risk. Add the missing capture fields and stage transitions to the operational systems. Build views that show aging, backlog, and exception counts at each workflow stage.

Partial workflow visibility creates false confidence. Reports show what is captured, not what is happening. The gaps — the work that falls between systems or between teams — are where risk accumulates. Closing specific blind spots rather than attempting to overhaul the entire architecture allows meaningful progress without a disruptive system redesign.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Enhance workflow analytics with stalled-work alerts, cycle-time views, delay analysis, and stage-level risk reporting.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Add analytical layers to existing workflow data: stalled-work detection, abnormal cycle-time alerts, stage-level risk classification, and delay attribution reporting. Configure views for managers that distinguish between normal workflow variation and genuine bottlenecks requiring intervention.

Strong workflow data becomes exponentially more valuable when it drives action rather than observation. At this maturity, the data exists but typically surfaces only what happened, not what needs to happen next. Adding diagnostic analytics — cycle time, delay attribution, stall detection — converts a reporting layer into an operational management tool.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Create an approved KPI dictionary covering exact formulas, inclusion rules, source fields, refresh cadence, owners, and targets for every management metric.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Create a KPI dictionary as a first-priority deliverable. For each metric that appears in management reporting or operational dashboards, document the exact formula, the inclusion and exclusion rules, the source field, the time period assumption, the owner, and the approved refresh schedule. Retire or consolidate duplicate or inconsistent metric definitions. Get explicit sign-off from the finance and operations leads who use the metrics.

KPIs that mean different things to different people are not KPIs — they are estimates. When one team reports pipeline at 2.4x coverage and another team reports it at 1.8x, neither number is wrong per their own logic, but neither can be used for a reliable management decision. A KPI dictionary takes one to two weeks to produce for a typical business of this scale. The cost of not having one compounds every month in management time spent reconciling figures and rebuilding trust in the numbers.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Align reports, dashboards, and teams to one KPI logic set, with defined thresholds and named owners for review and action.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Identify the specific metrics where definitions, thresholds, or time-period assumptions are inconsistent across teams. Convene a single alignment session with the key metric owners, agree the canonical definition for each disputed metric, and update reports and dashboards to reflect the agreed standard. Designate an owner for each metric who is responsible for the definition and the data source going forward.

KPI inconsistency at the developing stage is usually a governance problem rather than a system problem. The system can produce any number with any logic — the fix is deciding which logic is correct and enforcing it consistently. This does not require a technology project. It requires a decision, documented ownership, and updated reporting.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Strengthen the KPI layer with leading indicators, benchmark bands, and clearer action thresholds for management use.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Add leading indicators alongside existing lagging metrics to give management earlier warning of performance shifts. Define benchmark bands for each KPI — green, amber, red thresholds — and ensure dashboards surface exception status visually so attention goes to the right metrics without requiring manual interpretation.

Strong KPI frameworks typically report well on what happened. The next maturity step is reporting on what is about to happen. Leading indicators — pipeline velocity, early churn signals, capacity utilisation trends — give management the window to act before problems become visible in lagging metrics. This requires only adding three to five carefully chosen leading metrics to an existing reporting framework, not a wholesale redesign.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Rebuild the reporting pack around controlled source data, critical management decisions, and exception-based views so leaders are not relying on manual or conflicting reports.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Rebuild the core management reporting pack around controlled data sources. Replace manual extracts and pivot-table compilations with reports drawing directly from the approved source systems. Reduce the report set to the decisions leadership needs to make, not the data that is available to pull. For each report, define the decision it supports, the owner who signs off on it, and the cadence at which it runs.

Manual reporting creates a compounding cost. Time is spent building it, time is spent checking it, and errors still get through because the process depends on human judgment at every step. The confidence damage when a report is wrong — even once — can last months. Rebuilding on controlled sources is not primarily a technology project; it is a process redesign that removes manual steps and replaces them with governed automation.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Simplify and standardise dashboards, report cadence, and version control so reporting is timely, consistent, and decision-relevant.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Standardise the reporting cadence, format, and ownership across leadership. Define which reports are weekly, monthly, and quarterly. Agree on a single version of the truth for each reporting object — one pipeline report, one billing report, one customer report — and deprecate competing versions. Implement version control for dashboards so changes are tracked and reversible.

Inconsistent reporting cadence and format forces leadership to spend time at every review establishing which number is current and correct. Standardising the pack and eliminating competing versions reduces that friction to zero and ensures that every leadership conversation starts from the same information base.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Improve management reporting with role-based layouts, proactive alerts, and clearer views of risk, backlog, and trend movement.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Add role-based reporting layers so different stakeholders receive the level of detail appropriate to their decisions. Implement proactive alerts for metric threshold breaches, risk events, or delivery exceptions so management is notified immediately rather than discovering issues at the next scheduled review.

At strong maturity, reporting is trusted but uniform. Role-based views and proactive alerting make reporting active rather than passive — management stops scanning reports for problems and starts receiving targeted signals about the specific issues that require their attention.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Assign explicit ownership for data quality, KPI governance, dashboard logic, and reporting sign-off, supported by SOPs, review cadence, and correction workflows.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Appoint explicit data owners for each critical domain — pipeline data, customer data, billing data, KPI definitions, dashboard logic. Document their responsibilities in a one-page governance charter: what they own, how they maintain it, how issues are escalated, and how often standards are reviewed. Run the first formal governance review within 30 days of appointments being made.

Data governance without named ownership is a policy document, not a control. The most common reason governance frameworks fail is that they define standards but do not assign the accountability for enforcing them to a specific person who can be held responsible when things go wrong. Appointing owners and giving them a simple, actionable charter is the fastest path from weak governance to working governance.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Formalise governance routines, escalation paths, and control checks so standards are enforced consistently across teams.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Formalise the governance review cadence. Run monthly data quality and KPI accuracy reviews attended by the named domain owners and their manager. Establish an escalation path for unresolved issues so that persistent problems reach a decision-maker rather than cycling through informal discussions. Build change-control rules so that modifications to KPI definitions, source systems, or report logic require explicit sign-off before going live.

Governance at the developing stage typically exists in documents and occasional conversations but is not enforced through a regular, structured process. Without a review cadence and an escalation path, standards drift gradually as teams make local decisions that seem reasonable in isolation but collectively undermine the data framework.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Elevate governance maturity through control tracking, recurring review of unresolved issues, and structured continuous improvement.
recommendations.recommendation_detail:
Build a governance tracking register that records open issues, remediation owners, resolution dates, and recurring control checks. Run quarterly governance retrospectives that review the volume and type of issues raised, identify systemic root causes, and update standards accordingly. Use the retrospectives to drive a continuous improvement cycle rather than treating governance as a set-and-forget framework.

At strong maturity, governance is largely working. The risk at this level is complacency — standards that were designed for the current scale and system configuration become stale as the business grows. A continuous improvement cycle tied to actual issue tracking ensures governance evolves with the business rather than becoming a maintenance overhead.

actions.action_summary: Inventory every system, spreadsheet, and report currently used to produce pipeline, customer, billing, and management numbers.
actions.expected_outcome: Full view of the real reporting landscape

actions.action_summary: Define and approve the primary source of truth for each critical object and KPI, including who owns it and who may change it.
Retire, lock down, or formally control shadow spreadsheets that overwrite or replace CRM, finance, or service data.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduced reporting conflict and shadow logic

actions.action_summary: Map every critical KPI back to its source object, field set, refresh logic, and reconciliation rule.
Align field definitions across CRM, finance, service, and reporting layers so records reconcile without manual remapping.
actions.expected_outcome: Lower reconciliation effort and fewer mapping errors

actions.action_summary: Document architecture, data lineage, reconciliation logic, and KPI source ownership in a controlled reference file.
actions.expected_outcome: Better maintainability of the data foundation

actions.action_summary: Review sync logic, reconciliation breaks, and integration ownership between core systems on a scheduled basis.
Make critical stage-based fields mandatory and define minimum completion standards for pipeline, billing, and customer records.
actions.expected_outcome: Higher completeness at the point of work

actions.action_summary: Introduce clear stage-exit criteria and update SLAs for status, owner, amount, date, and next-step fields.
Run weekly hygiene checks for incomplete, overdue, stale, or ownerless records and escalate repeat offenders.
Train teams on the exact capture rules that affect reporting accuracy, including common failure fields and handoff updates.
Create a compliance view showing missing fields, late updates, stale stages, and completion rates by owner or team.
Add reminders, exception flags, or workflow prompts for overdue updates in high-volume record types.
Review capture-quality trends quarterly and tighten controls where teams or workflows show slippage.
Run a focused cleanup of duplicates, stale records, missing values, broken links, and inconsistent categories across core objects.
Implement validation rules, duplicate-prevention controls, and taxonomy standards in the primary systems.
Establish a recurring monthly data-quality review covering completeness, freshness, duplicate risk, and classification issues.
Set measurable quality thresholds for completeness, freshness, duplication, and category consistency by record type.
Assign a steward for each major record type to monitor quality trends and resolve repeat error patterns.
Add exception reporting for abnormal values, broken record links, unusual stage movement, and category drift.
Introduce anomaly and enrichment checks for critical master-data fields such as customer type, product, region, and status.
actions.expected_outcome: Higher resilience as the dataset scales

actions.action_summary: Map the actual lifecycle stages and cross-functional handoffs, then align system fields and statuses to those real steps.
Add timestamp, handoff, queue, backlog, and drop-off markers where lifecycle visibility currently breaks.
Build a base view of stage movement, aging, rework, and backlog for management review.
Add missing fields or statuses for handoff completion, queue state, onboarding progression, and other blind spots.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduced blind spots in workflow reporting

actions.action_summary: Introduce aging, delay, drop-off, and rework reporting across critical revenue and service workflows.
actions.expected_outcome: Better operational insight into where flow breaks

actions.action_summary: Create exception views for stalled work, delayed handoffs, unusual cycle time, and abnormal stage dwell.
Add comparative views for stage conversion, cycle time, backlog movement, and rework trends.
Create a KPI dictionary that records exact formulas, inclusion rules, source fields, cadence, targets, and owners.
actions.expected_outcome: One approved logic set for management KPIs

actions.action_summary: Approve targets, threshold bands, and escalation triggers for the KPIs used in management meetings.
Align all dashboards, spreadsheets, and management packs to the approved KPI definitions and period logic.
Review current reports for formula, period, and threshold inconsistency and correct them against the approved logic.
Assign KPI owners who validate the number, explain movement, and trigger corrective action where needed.
Add leading indicators and early-warning thresholds to complement lagging performance measures.
Introduce benchmark bands or target ranges so KPI performance can be interpreted consistently by leaders and managers.
Remove redundant reports and rebuild the core management pack around trusted source data and decision-critical metrics.
actions.expected_outcome: Cleaner and more reliable management reporting

actions.action_summary: Automate dashboard and report builds from approved source data instead of manual spreadsheet manipulation where possible.
Add exception views for stage risk, stale records, billing backlog, missing data, and other management triggers.
actions.expected_outcome: Reporting that drives action, not just visibility

actions.action_summary: Standardise dashboard layouts, refresh cadence, naming, and version control across stakeholder groups.
Simplify report preparation by removing low-value tabs, duplicate views, and unnecessary manual transformation steps.
actions.expected_outcome: Faster and more consistent reporting cycle

actions.action_summary: Create role-based views so leaders, managers, and operators each see the metrics and exceptions relevant to them.
Add alerting or early-warning indicators for key operational, billing, customer, and data-quality risks.
Assign named owners for data quality, KPI definition changes, dashboard logic, report preparation, and final sign-off.
Document SOPs for data capture, field changes, KPI updates, dashboard changes, and reporting corrections.
actions.expected_outcome: Controlled operating rules for the data layer

actions.action_summary: Establish a recurring monthly governance review for data issues, KPI exceptions, and unresolved reporting defects.
Formalise escalation and correction rules for repeat data, KPI, or reporting failures, including who approves fixes.
Use a governance checklist to review ownership, control status, open issues, KPI changes, and dashboard updates.
Run quarterly governance reviews focused on trend analysis, recurring control exceptions, and improvement priorities.
Track governance actions, unresolved issues, and control decisions in a visible log with owners and due dates.
